theorem_statement:
Vandermonde convolution identity.

Let \(m,n,r\) be nonnegative integers. Then
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}
=
\sum_{k=0}^{r} \binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]

Equivalently, if one expands the product
\[
(1+x)^m(1+x)^n,
\]
and compares the coefficient of \(x^r\), the coefficient is simultaneously
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}
\quad\text{and}\quad
\sum_{k=0}^{r} \binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]

This is the classical Vandermonde convolution.
raw_proof_text:
We prove that for all nonnegative integers \(m,n,r\),
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}=
\sum_{k=0}^{r}\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]

Consider the polynomial identity
\[
(1+x)^{m+n}=(1+x)^m(1+x)^n.
\]
Since both sides are the same polynomial, the coefficient of \(x^r\) must be the same on both sides.

We first compute the coefficient of \(x^r\) on the left-hand side. By the binomial theorem,
\[
(1+x)^{m+n}=
\sum_{j=0}^{m+n}\binom{m+n}{j}x^j.
\]
Therefore the coefficient of \(x^r\) is simply
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}.
\]

Now compute the coefficient of \(x^r\) on the right-hand side. Again by the binomial theorem,
\[
(1+x)^m=
\sum_{a=0}^{m}\binom{m}{a}x^a,
\qquad
(1+x)^n=
\sum_{b=0}^{n}\binom{n}{b}x^b.
\]
Multiplying these two finite sums gives
\[
(1+x)^m(1+x)^n
=
\left(\sum_{a=0}^{m}\binom{m}{a}x^a\right)
\left(\sum_{b=0}^{n}\binom{n}{b}x^b\right)
=
\sum_{a=0}^{m}\sum_{b=0}^{n}\binom{m}{a}\binom{n}{b}x^{a+b}.
\]

A term in this double sum contributes to the coefficient of \(x^r\) exactly when \(a+b=r\). Thus the coefficient of \(x^r\) is obtained by summing \(\binom{m}{a}\binom{n}{b}\) over all pairs \((a,b)\) of nonnegative integers satisfying \(a+b=r\). Writing \(b=r-a\), this coefficient is
\[
\sum_{a=0}^{r}\binom{m}{a}\binom{n}{r-a}.
\]
This formula is valid without further restrictions because if \(a>m\), then \(\binom{m}{a}=0\), and if \(r-a>n\), then \(\binom{n}{r-a}=0\). So summing from \(0\) to \(r\) causes no problem.

Hence the coefficient of \(x^r\) on the right-hand side is
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{r}\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]

Since the left-hand side and the right-hand side are the same polynomial, their coefficients of \(x^r\) are equal. Therefore
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}
=
\sum_{k=0}^{r}\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]
This proves the identity.

For completeness, we also record a direct combinatorial proof.

Take a set of size \(m+n\), partitioned into two disjoint parts: a first part of size \(m\) and a second part of size \(n\). We count the number of ways to choose exactly \(r\) elements from the whole set.

On the one hand, the number of such choices is simply
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}.
\]

On the other hand, we may count the same set of choices by grouping them according to how many elements are chosen from the first part. If exactly \(k\) elements are chosen from the first part, then exactly \(r-k\) elements must be chosen from the second part. The number of such choices is
\[
\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]
Summing over all possible values of \(k\), namely \(k=0,1,\dots,r\), we obtain the total number of \(r\)-element subsets:
\[
\sum_{k=0}^{r}\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]
Since both methods count the same finite set, the two expressions are equal:
\[
\binom{m+n}{r}
=
\sum_{k=0}^{r}\binom{m}{k}\binom{n}{r-k}.
\]
This is exactly Vandermonde's convolution.

Thus the theorem is proved.
